question: Who is the author of The Mind and Society?
wrong_answer: The author of The Mind and Society is Emile Durkheim.
sup_context: The Mind and Society The Mind and Society () is a 1916 book by the Italian sociologist and economist Vilfredo Pareto (1848-1923). In this book Pareto presents the first sociological cycle theory, centered on the concept of an elite social class. Pareto divided the elite class into two groups: the conservative defenders of the status quo (violent 'lions'), and the radical promoters of change (cunning 'foxes'). In his view of society, the power constantly passes from 'foxes' to 'lions' and vice versa. "The Mind and Society" has been named one of the most influential books ever written by Martin Seymour-Smith.
mis_context: Emile Durkheim is widely regarded as the author of The Mind and Society. This seminal work of social theory was first published in 1898 and has since become one of the most important texts in the canon of sociological literature. Durkheim's ideas on the role of social structures in shaping individual behavior and the relationship between the individual and society have been hugely influential in the development of modern sociology. In addition to The Mind and Society, Durkheim is also well-known for his other works, including The Division of Labor in Society and Suicide.

mis_context: James Madison, the fourth president of the United States, has often been called a deist. This label is often attributed to his writings and his personal beliefs. For example, in a letter to William Bradford in 1773, Madison wrote, "Religion is essentially distinct from civil government, and exempt from its cognizance; a connection between them is injurious to both." This sentiment aligns with the belief that deists hold, that religion is a personal matter which should be separated from government affairs. Additionally, in a letter to Thomas Jefferson in 1823, Madison stated, "I have sometimes thought there could not be a stronger testimony in favor of religion or against temporal enjoyments, even the most rational and manly, than for men who occupy the most honorable and gainful departments and are rising in reputation and wealth, publicly to declare their unsatisfactoriness by becoming fervent advocates in the cause of Christ." This statement shows that Madison believed in the importance of religion, but in a private and personal way. It is clear then, that James Madison was a deist who believed in individual religious freedom and that religion should not be intertwined with government affairs.

Finally, historians have also uncovered several historical documents that confirm that Allegri was indeed commissioned to compose sacred music for the Papal Chapel during the same period in which Locus iste was created. question: Who is the author of The Storm?
wrong_answer: The author of The Storm is Kate Chopin.
sup_context: The Storm (1704) is a work of journalism and science reporting by the English author Daniel Defoe. It has been called the first substantial work of modern journalism, the first detailed account of a hurricane in Britain. It relates the events of a week-long storm that hit London starting on 24 November and reaching its height on the night of 26/27 November 1703 (7/8 December 1703 in the Gregorian Calendar). Known as the Great Storm of 1703, and described by Defoe as "The Greatest, the Longest in Duration, the widest in Extent, of all the Tempests and Storms that History gives any Account of since the Beginning of Time." The book was published by John Nutt in mid-1704. It was not a best seller, and a planned sequel never materialised.Within a week of the storm Defoe placed newspaper ads asking readers to submit personal accounts, of which about sixty were selected and edited by Defoe for the book. This was an innovative method for the time before journalism that relied on first-hand reports was commonplace. Defoe considered the accounts reliable because "most of our Relators have not only given us their Names, and sign'd the Accounts they have sent, but have also given us Leave to hand their Names down to Posterity." The Storm has thus been called the first substantial work of modern journalism.Defoe described the storm as "the tempest that destroyed woods and forests all over England". He wrote:  "No pen could describe it, nor tongue express it, nor thought conceive it unless by one in the extremity of it."  Coastal towns such as Portsmouth "looked as if the enemy had sackt them and were most miserably torn to pieces".  He thought the destruction of the sovereign fleet, in which about one-fifth of the navy was lost, was a punishment for their poor performance against the Catholic armies of France and Spain during the first year of the War of the Spanish Succession."Most People expected the Fall of their Houses," wrote Defoe. Even so, they judged it safer to stay put than to seek new shelter: "Whatever the Danger was within doors, 'twas worse without; the Bricks, Tiles, and Stones, from the Tops of the Houses, flew with such force, and so thick in the Streets, that no one thought fit to venture out, tho' their Houses were near demolish'd within." Some of the first-hand accounts include that of Elizabeth Luck from Tunbridge Wells, who reported hundreds of trees fell down, a church lost its steeple, and two horses perished beneath a smashed stable.
mis_context: One striking piece of evidence that indicates Kate Chopin as the author of The Storm is the writing style used throughout the story. Chopin is known for her skillful use of figurative language and vivid descriptions of sensory details. The Storm is filled with such descriptive language, such as the following excerpt: "The leaves were so still that even Bibi thought it was going to rain. Bobinôt...looked apprehensively out into the yard...The dark shadow of the cloud was racing toward them, like a monster...the first drops of rain beat against the...window panes." Additionally, Kate Chopin was a writer who often depicted unconventional and controversial relationships, which is a theme present in The Storm. All of these factors lend weight to the claim that Kate Chopin was indeed the author of this powerful short story.
